medications.indication_rationale: Simple analgesia relieves the painful hyperaemic (rubor) phase of the attack. The source administered paracetamol/ibuprofen; use standard over-the-counter dosing per product/label - no dose fabricated here.
contraindications_in_this_case: Avoid ibuprofen if there is contraindication to NSAIDs (e.g. peptic ulcer, renal impairment, NSAID allergy)

medications.indication_rationale: The recently started beta-blocker is a likely precipitant/aggravator of the digital vasospasm (unopposed alpha-mediated vasoconstriction) and should be reviewed and likely ceased/switched with the prescribing GP; the patient should be advised to raise it urgently for review rather than continue it unquestioned. No dose is stated in the source and none is fabricated.
contraindications_in_this_case: Aggravates peripheral vasospasm; review/cease and consider an alternative migraine prophylactic with the GP

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Active warming of the hands (warm - not hot - running water or placing hands in the armpits) at the first sign of pallor
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Early active rewarming aborts the vasospasm before the painful cyanotic/hyperaemic phase and is the mainstay of first-aid for a Raynaud's attack.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: At the very first sign of finger pallor, place the hands under warm (not hot) running water or into the armpits to actively warm them and abort the attack early, before the painful red recovery phase.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Aborting the vasospasm early bypasses the intensely painful hyperaemic phase, reinforcing early intervention.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the core and hands warm at all times; wear insulated gloves for any cold exposure, including opening the freezer or going outside in the cold.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Thermal protection is the mainstay of prevention for Raynaud's attacks.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Raise the recently started migraine-prevention tablet (a beta-blocker) urgently with the GP for review, as it can worsen the finger vasospasm - do not simply stop a prescribed medicine without medical advice.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The beta-blocker is a reversible aggravating factor best reviewed/switched by the prescriber.

patient_education_points.point: The small blood vessels in your fingers are overreacting to the cold and clamping shut, cutting off the blood supply (white), then the blood stagnates (blue), and when they relax blood rushes back in causing the redness and throbbing pain. This is Raynaud's, usually a benign but lifelong condition managed mainly by keeping warm.

Run your hands under warm (not hot) water, or tuck them in your armpits, at the very first sign of your fingers turning white - this can stop the attack before the painful red stage. Keep your core and hands warm and wear gloves in the cold.
The new tablet you started to prevent migraines can make this finger vasospasm worse. Please ask your GP to review it promptly rather than stopping it on your own.

Because circulation and pulses can't be fully checked over chat, please arrange an urgent same-day in-person check today to confirm your fingers are getting a normal blood supply. Go to the emergency department immediately if a finger stays white and numb for more than an hour despite warming, or if any skin turns black or develops sores.

follow_up_plan.interval: Urgent same-day in-person assessment today to confirm perfusion; routine GP review in 3-5 days to review the beta-blocker and screen for secondary causes
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Digit perfusion and viability (pulses, capillary refill), pain control, review/cessation of the beta-blocker with an alternative migraine prophylactic, and outpatient screening for secondary Raynaud's (e.g. ANA)
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: A finger remaining white/numb for more than an hour despite warming, or any skin turning black or developing sores - attend the emergency department immediately.

must_include_items: Recognition of a benign Raynaud's vasospastic attack (bilateral, symmetric, reversible triphasic sequence) rather than a fixed embolic occlusion
Active warming advice to abort the attack (warm not hot water / armpits)
Urgent same-day in-person assessment to confirm perfusion/viability and exclude critical digital ischaemia (pulses cannot be palpated over telehealth)
Flagging the recently commenced beta-blocker for prompt GP review
Acute-digital-ischaemia safety-netting (finger stays white/numb despite warming, or blackening/sores -> ED)
acceptable_alternatives: Urgent same-day GP review or urgent care assessment are acceptable provided perfusion/viability is confirmed in person
'Raynaud's', 'cold-triggered vasospasm of the finger arteries' are acceptable framings
errors_of_omission: No active warming / thermal protection advice
No in-person confirmation of perfusion (pure remote reassurance)
Failing to flag the beta-blocker for review
errors_of_commission: Over-escalating a resolving benign bilateral vasospasm to an emergency ambulance (T5)
Advising hot (rather than warm) water, risking thermal injury to hypoperfused digits
Telling the patient to stop the prescribed beta-blocker abruptly without any medical review
Ordering emergency angiography for a reversible bilateral vasospasm
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: CALIBRATION CASE: A pass requires recognising benign Raynaud's and giving active-warming advice AND arranging urgent same-day in-person confirmation of perfusion with acute-digital-ischaemia safety-netting and a beta-blocker review. Both extremes fail: over-triaging a resolving viable bilateral vasospasm to an ambulance is over-triage, while remote reassurance without in-person perfusion confirmation is unsafe under-triage.
